title: The Time Is Ripe for Russia-Ukraine Peace Talks, But Putin Could Escalate Conflict | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Graham argues the Western narrative that the tide has turned toward Ukraine is premature: despite Ukrainian deep strikes crippling Russian fuel supply and a looming Russian budget crisis, Putin has expanded his maximalist demands to include Novorossiya, and only US-led shuttle diplomacy can broker a realistic framework (ceasefire on the line of contact, no NATO membership or eastward expansion).

insights.claim: Ukraine has halted Russia's battlefield advances, and its deep strikes on Russian energy infrastructure have caused a nationwide gasoline shortage and reduced Russian oil exports that provide critical revenue for the war machine.
insights.speaker: Thomas Graham
insights.so_what: Ukrainian strikes are a live disruptor of Russian oil-export revenue and domestic fuel supply—a channel to watch for pressure on Russian output and prices.

insights.claim: Russia's economy is on the edge of a recession with a budget crisis looming, gasoline shortages mounting, popular and elite discontent with the war growing, and Putin's popularity rating steadily falling.
insights.speaker: Thomas Graham
insights.so_what: Mounting Russian economic and political strain is the pressure that could eventually force Moscow to the table—a key macro signal for Russia-exposed risk.
insights.locator: The gasoline shortages are only one manifestation

insights.claim: A well-executed Ukrainian drone campaign is isolating Russian-annexed Crimea from the rest of Russia.
insights.speaker: Thomas Graham
insights.so_what: Crimea's isolation is a tangible Ukrainian gain that strengthens Kyiv's negotiating hand.
insights.locator: A well-executed campaign of drone strikes

themes.theme: Why the tide has not actually turned

insights.claim: It is premature to say the tide is turning in Ukraine's favor: Kyiv faces a deepening demographic crisis, growing economic havoc, and mounting reconstruction costs, and needs to end the conflict as soon as possible.
insights.speaker: Thomas Graham
insights.so_what: Counters the consensus optimism—Ukraine's structural weakness argues for a near-term settlement rather than prolonged fighting.
insights.quote: it is premature to say that the tide is turning in Ukraine's favor
insights.locator: Nevertheless, it is premature to say

insights.claim: Rather than moving toward negotiations, Putin has expanded his maximalist demands to include liberating not just the Donbas but also Novorossiya—a historical designation stretching from Kharkiv province to Moldova and Transnistria—while stepping up massive air strikes exploiting gaps in Ukraine's air defenses.
insights.speaker: Thomas Graham
insights.so_what: Expanding, not softening, demands signals Putin sees no reason to settle yet—raising the odds of escalation over near-term peace.
insights.locator: Putin is not backing away from his maximalist demands.

insights.claim: Trump's decision to license Patriot interceptor production to Ukraine will do nothing to close its air-defense gap in the near term, because Ukraine will need months, if not years, before it can produce the interceptors locally.
insights.speaker: Thomas Graham
insights.so_what: Tempers the market/political read of the Patriot announcement—no near-term change to Ukraine's vulnerability to Russian air strikes.

themes.theme: Diplomacy: Washington's leverage and Putin's aims

insights.claim: Only Washington can lead a settlement effort because it alone can talk to all parties—Moscow, Kyiv, and key European capitals—while the Europeans' inability to agree on an approach and Moscow's skepticism of their strength doom a Europe-led channel.
insights.speaker: Thomas Graham
insights.so_what: Frames the US as the indispensable broker—any peace path runs through Washington-led shuttle diplomacy, not European initiative.
insights.locator: Intensive diplomacy is urgently needed now

insights.claim: Putin initiated two phone calls to Trump in April and May to discuss Ukraine, but Graham is skeptical Putin wants Washington as a good-faith mediator—more likely his goal is to counter Europe's and Ukraine's growing influence on Trump.
insights.speaker: Thomas Graham
insights.so_what: Warns that Russian diplomatic overtures may be a wedge tactic rather than genuine peace-seeking—discount Kremlin engagement signals accordingly.
insights.locator: In the past two months, Moscow has made clear

insights.claim: US leverage over Ukraine is diminishing as Kyiv produces more of its own weaponry, but Kyiv still relies on the US for critical battlefield intelligence and urgently needs more Patriot interceptors.
insights.speaker: Thomas Graham
insights.so_what: Identifies intelligence and air-defense dependence as Washington's remaining pressure points over Kyiv—the levers in any deal.
insights.locator: Its leverage over Ukraine might be diminishing

themes.theme: The likely framework of a deal

insights.claim: The contours of a framework are already visible: a ceasefire along the line of contact, closer Ukraine-West security ties without formal NATO membership, and agreement on no further NATO expansion eastward—an outcome both Kyiv and Moscow could claim as victory.
insights.speaker: Thomas Graham
insights.so_what: A concrete, tradeable base-case settlement shape—frozen line of contact plus NATO-membership foreclosure is the realistic endgame to price.
insights.locator: Despite the sharpening rhetoric on all sides

insights.claim: Any Russia-Ukraine settlement would be fragile and is only the first step in a longer negotiation over European security that could stretch out for years, if not decades.
insights.speaker: Thomas Graham
insights.so_what: Even a ceasefire leaves a prolonged period of European security instability—no clean 'peace dividend' repricing for years.
insights.locator: Such an agreement would be fragile.
